+{"id":"bd-17yyv","title":"Latency and SLO Dashboard with Alerts","description":"## Overview

Add latency/SLO monitoring for key APIs and agent operations with visual dashboards and alerting when SLOs are violated.

## Background and Rationale

Performance is critical for multi-agent workflows. Without explicit SLO tracking, regressions go unnoticed. This feature provides clear, actionable performance health.

## Scope

- Collect latency metrics (p50/p95/p99)
- Define SLOs per endpoint and operation
- UI dashboard with trends and burn rate
- Alerts on SLO breach

## SLO Model

```typescript
interface SloDefinition {
  id: string;
  target: string; // e.g., GET /api/agents
  objectiveMs: number; // p95 threshold
  window: '1h' | '6h' | '24h';
}
```

## API

- `GET /api/slo`
- `POST /api/slo`
- `GET /api/slo/status`

## Implementation Tasks

1. Add latency metrics collection in middleware
2. Store rolling latency stats
3. Implement SLO evaluator + burn rate
4. Build UI dashboard (charts + status)
5. Hook alerts into notification system

## Testing

- Unit: SLO evaluator and burn rate
- Integration: latency stats aggregation
- E2E: SLO breach triggers alert

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Dashboard displays p50/p95 trends
- [ ] SLO breaches are detected within 5 minutes
- [ ] Alerts fire with clear context
- [ ] Users can define custom SLOs","notes":"Testing note: E2E coverage should use the existing Playwright TestLogger/StructuredReporter to capture console, network, WS, and traces; emit deterministic log bundles for debugging.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:32:24.800775656Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T03:36:16.151504208Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-17yyv","depends_on_id":"bd-3c0o3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:33:13.298551238Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

+{"id":"bd-1b6lz","title":"Safety Policy Simulator","description":"## Overview

Create a simulator that predicts how DCG/SLB/UBS will treat a command or change before it runs, providing a safe "what-if" evaluation.

## Background and Rationale

Operators want to know if a command will be blocked without triggering an actual block or audit event. A simulator improves developer velocity and reduces false positives.

## Scope

- Simulation API for commands and file operations
- Policy snapshotting for deterministic results
- UI panel to test commands
- Integration with DCG + UBS rules

## API

- `POST /api/safety/simulate` → returns allowed/blocked + reason
- `GET /api/safety/rules` → show active rules

## Implementation Tasks

1. Add simulator engine that reuses DCG rule evaluation
2. Create policy snapshot mechanism (versioned)
3. Expose simulate endpoint + rule list
4. Build UI panel for simulation
5. Add metrics: simulation count, block reasons

## Testing

- Unit: simulator uses same rule outputs as DCG
- Integration: simulation matches actual block behavior
- E2E: UI simulation flow

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Simulation matches real DCG outcomes
- [ ] No actual block events are recorded during simulation
- [ ] Results include clear rationale and suggested fixes","notes":"Testing note: E2E coverage should use the existing Playwright TestLogger/StructuredReporter to capture console, network, WS, and traces; emit deterministic log bundles for debugging.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:31:41.489557563Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T03:35:50.352933928Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

-{"id":"bd-1efrp","title":"Support Bundle and Diagnostics Export","description":"## Overview

Add a one-click Support Bundle that gathers logs, configuration (sanitized), recent events, and system diagnostics into a portable archive for debugging issues quickly.

## Background and Rationale

When agents misbehave, support triage is slow because data is scattered across logs, DB tables, and UI views. A structured bundle speeds incident response and provides reproducible context without exposing secrets.

## Scope

- Data capture pipeline (logs, config, metrics, events)
- Redaction rules and secret scrubbing
- Export format + download API
- UI entry point and progress tracking
- Audit record of bundle creation

## Bundle Contents (Default)

- Gateway version/build info
- Recent logs (last N minutes, selectable)
- Agent session summaries (last N sessions)
- WebSocket event snapshots
- Health/metrics summary (CPU/mem, latency, error rates)
- Routing/driver info + active pools
- DCG block stats + recent incidents
- .beads/ summary (counts only, no private details)

## Security and Redaction

- Strip API keys, tokens, secrets
- Hash or truncate IDs where needed
- Allow per-field redaction rules
- Include a redaction report in bundle

## API

- `POST /api/support/bundles` (create, returns job id)
- `GET /api/support/bundles/:id/status`
- `GET /api/support/bundles/:id/download`

## UI

- Support page with "Create Support Bundle"
- Checkboxes to include/exclude data sources
- Progress indicator + completion notification

## Implementation Tasks

1. Define bundle manifest schema
2. Implement redaction engine with tests
3. Build bundle generator service
4. Add async job runner + storage
5. Expose API endpoints for create/status/download
6. Add UI flow and progress view
7. Add audit log entry on bundle creation

## Testing

- Unit: redaction rules + manifest serialization
- Integration: bundle creation, status polling, download
- E2E: UI flow, verify sensitive fields are removed

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Bundle can be generated and downloaded via API
- [ ] Redaction removes all secrets consistently
- [ ] Bundle is complete and reproducible
- [ ] Audit log records bundle creation event
- [ ] UI provides clear progress and result status","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:29:50.526555158Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T03:29:50.526555158Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

+{"id":"bd-1weoa","title":"Workspace Cost Caps and Auto-Throttle","description":"## Overview

Enforce hard and soft cost caps per workspace with automatic throttling actions (pause new sessions, downgrade models, or queue requests) when budgets are exceeded.

## Background and Rationale

Cost analytics exists, but there is no enforcement layer. Teams need predictable cost control, especially for multi-agent workloads that can spike unexpectedly. Automated throttling prevents runaway spend without manual intervention.

## Scope

- Budget policies (soft + hard caps)
- Enforcement actions and escalation
- API + UI for configuration
- Audit logs and alert integration

## Policy Model

```typescript
interface CostCapPolicy {
  workspaceId: string;
  period: 'daily' | 'weekly' | 'monthly';
  softCapUsd: number;
  hardCapUsd: number;
  actions: {
    onSoftCap: 'notify' | 'downgrade' | 'throttle';
    onHardCap: 'pause' | 'queue' | 'downgrade';
  };
}
```

## Enforcement Actions

- **notify**: emit alerts + notification
- **downgrade**: route to cheaper model (integrate with routing policies)
- **throttle**: limit new agent starts per minute
- **pause**: block new sessions until period resets or override
- **queue**: accept requests but defer execution

## API

- `GET /api/cost-caps`
- `PUT /api/cost-caps`
- `POST /api/cost-caps/override` (temporary override)

## UI

- Budget settings panel
- Real-time progress toward caps
- Action logs + override controls

## Implementation Tasks

1. Add cost cap policy schema + storage
2. Implement cost cap evaluator (period rollups)
3. Enforce actions in session creation pipeline
4. Integrate with model router for downgrade action
5. Add alert events + notifications
6. Build UI panel with status and override

## Testing

- Unit: cap evaluation by period
- Integration: hard cap blocks session start
- Integration: soft cap triggers downgrade
- E2E: configure cap, run simulated spend, verify UI + behavior

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Caps enforce consistently across requests
- [ ] Actions are auditable and reversible
- [ ] UI shows real-time status and overrides
- [ ] Soft cap actions do not block existing sessions","notes":"Testing note: E2E coverage should use the existing Playwright TestLogger/StructuredReporter to capture console, network, WS, and traces; emit deterministic log bundles for debugging.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:30:26.049171310Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T03:35:07.096557810Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

+{"id":"bd-2d0a3","title":"Event Streaming API (Webhook and SSE)","description":"## Overview

Expose a durable event stream via Webhooks and Server-Sent Events so external systems can react to agent activity in real time.

## Background and Rationale

WebSocket is great for the UI but not for external automation. A signed webhook/SSE stream enables CI/CD integrations, alerting, and custom analytics.

## Scope

- Event subscription management
- Signed webhook delivery with retries
- SSE endpoint for live streaming
- Event schemas and versioning

## API

- `POST /api/events/subscriptions` (webhook)
- `DELETE /api/events/subscriptions/:id`
- `GET /api/events/stream` (SSE)

## Webhook Delivery

- HMAC signature with secret
- Retry with exponential backoff
- Dead-letter queue after N failures

## Implementation Tasks

1. Define event schema + versioning
2. Build subscription store
3. Implement webhook dispatcher + retries
4. Add SSE endpoint with backfill support
5. UI for managing subscriptions

## Testing

- Unit: signature validation + retry logic
- Integration: webhook delivery and DLQ
- E2E: create subscription and receive events

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Webhooks are signed and retried reliably
- [ ] SSE streams deliver events in order
- [ ] Subscriptions manageable via UI and API

-{"id":"bd-3gwij","title":"Model Routing and Failover Policies","description":"## Overview

Introduce a policy-driven model router that chooses the best provider/model per request (cost, latency, quality, availability, workspace budgets), with automatic failover and cooldown rules when a model degrades.

## Background and Rationale

Flywheel already supports BYOA and multiple providers, but selection is mostly static. Teams need predictable, auditable routing that:
- optimizes for cost/latency/quality goals
- enforces workspace budgets and SLAs
- fails over deterministically when a provider is unhealthy
- explains **why** a model was chosen

This feature makes model usage reliable and intentional rather than ad hoc.

## Scope

- Policy evaluation engine (routing + failover)
- Persistence for policies and routing decisions
- APIs to manage and audit policies
- UI for policy creation and live decision traces
- Metrics + alerts when failover triggers

## Policy Model

```typescript
interface RoutingPolicy {
  id: string;
  name: string;
  priority: number; // lower wins
  enabled: boolean;
  match: {
    workspaceId?: string;
    agentTags?: Record<string, string>;
    taskType?: string;
    minContextHeadroom?: number; // percent
    timeOfDay?: { start: string; end: string; tz: string };
  };
  route: {
    preferred: Array<{ provider: 'openai' | 'anthropic' | 'google'; model: string; weight?: number }>;
    maxCostPer1kTokens?: number;
    maxP95LatencyMs?: number;
  };
  failover: {
    maxAttempts: number; // per request
    onErrors: Array<'rate_limit' | 'timeout' | '5xx' | 'tool_error'>;
    cooldownMinutes: number; // model-level circuit breaker
  };
}
```

## Key Components

1. **ModelRouterService**
   - Evaluate policies in priority order
   - Select primary model (weighted or deterministic)
   - Apply failover with circuit breaker and cooldown

2. **RoutingPolicyStore**
   - CRUD policies
   - Version history and audit trail

3. **Decision Trace**
   - Record routing decisions with inputs and evaluation path
   - Expose trace to UI and API

4. **Health Signals**
   - Use existing provider health + latency metrics
   - Feed into routing constraints

## API Additions

- `GET /api/routing/policies`
- `POST /api/routing/policies`
- `PUT /api/routing/policies/:id`
- `POST /api/routing/policies/:id/disable`
- `GET /api/routing/decisions?agentId=...`

## UI

- Policy list + builder (rules, constraints, failover)
- Live decision trace view (why model chosen)
- Model health + cooldown status display

## Database

```sql
CREATE TABLE model_routing_policies (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  name TEXT NOT NULL,
  priority INTEGER NOT NULL,
  enabled INTEGER NOT NULL,
  policy_json TEXT NOT NULL,
  created_at TEXT NOT NULL,
  updated_at TEXT NOT NULL
);

CREATE TABLE model_routing_decisions (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  agent_id TEXT,
  policy_id TEXT,
  provider TEXT,
  model TEXT,
  decision_trace TEXT,
  created_at TEXT NOT NULL
);
```

## Implementation Tasks

1. Add routing policy schema + storage
2. Implement ModelRouterService with policy evaluation
3. Add circuit breaker + cooldown tracking per model
4. Persist routing decision traces
5. Expose CRUD APIs + decision query
6. Add UI policy builder and decision trace viewer
7. Add metrics: route count, failover count, cooldown events
8. Integrate with existing provider health checks

## Testing

- Unit: policy matching, priority ordering, weighted selection
- Unit: failover rules + cooldown
- Integration: failover triggers on simulated provider errors
- E2E: create policy and verify routing decisions in UI

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Requests choose models based on explicit policies
- [ ] Failover occurs on configured error types
- [ ] Cooldown prevents flapping to unhealthy models
- [ ] Decision traces are visible and searchable
- [ ] Policies are versioned and auditable","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:29:32.156806352Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T03:29:32.156806352Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

-{"id":"bd-67lwz","title":"Agent Session Replay and Timeline","description":"## Overview
Provide a first-class, queryable timeline of agent activity (state transitions, tool calls/results, outputs, errors, file ops) with a UI replay mode. This turns today’s scattered logs into a single narrative that can be audited, debugged, and shared.

## Why This Is Accretive
- Debugging and incident response become dramatically faster.
- Users can replay exactly what an agent did and why a failure occurred.
- Provides the foundation for analytics, SLA verification, and governance.

## Scope and Design
- Persist normalized agent events with stable schemas and redacted payloads.
- Provide cursor-based API access for efficient playback.
- UI timeline with filtering, grouping, and export.
- Retention and sampling controls for storage safety.

## Proposed API
- `GET /api/agents/:id/timeline?cursor=&limit=&types=`
- `GET /api/agents/:id/events/:eventId`
- `GET /api/agents/:id/timeline/export?format=json|csv`
- WebSocket channel `agent:{id}:timeline` (optional live feed)

## Data Model (SQLite)
```
agent_events (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  agent_id TEXT NOT NULL,
  type TEXT NOT NULL,          -- state, tool_call, tool_result, output, error, file
  severity TEXT,               -- info|warn|error
  occurred_at TEXT NOT NULL,
  payload TEXT NOT NULL,       -- JSON (redacted)
  correlation_id TEXT,
  session_id TEXT,
  created_at TEXT NOT NULL
)
CREATE INDEX idx_agent_events_agent_time ON agent_events(agent_id, occurred_at DESC);
```

## Implementation Plan
1. Add `agent_events` schema and migration.
2. Create `agent-event.service.ts` to normalize + store events.
3. Extend BaseDriver emit pipeline to forward events to service.
4. Add redaction layer for payloads (reuse audit redaction).
5. Implement retention policy (size and age based).
6. Build API routes with cursor pagination.
7. Add UI timeline page + filters + event detail drawer.
8. Add export endpoint and UI action.
9. Optional: WebSocket live feed for real-time replay.

## Testing
- Unit: event normalization, redaction, cursor pagination.
- Integration: event ingestion from driver state transitions.
- E2E: timeline page renders events and filters properly.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Timeline shows all key agent events in order.
- Cursor pagination is stable and deterministic.
- Payloads are redacted (no secrets in UI/export).
- Retention policy keeps DB bounded.

## Dependencies
- Existing driver event emission and audit redaction utilities.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:25:53.975454816Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T03:25:53.975454816Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"bd-66wud","title":"RBAC and API Key Scopes","description":"## Overview

Add role-based access control with scoped API keys so different users and automations can access only the features they need.

## Background and Rationale

As teams grow, a single admin role is insufficient. Operators need read-only dashboards, developers need limited agent access, and automation keys should be narrowly scoped to reduce blast radius.

## Scope

- Roles and permission matrix
- Scoped API keys (read/write, per-resource)
- UI for role assignment and key management
- Permission enforcement across API and WebSocket

## Roles (Initial)

- **Owner**: full access
- **Admin**: manage agents, policies, dashboards
- **Developer**: manage own agents + sessions
- **Viewer**: read-only
- **Automation**: scoped key-only access

## Permission Model

```typescript
type Permission =
```

## Why This Is Accretive
- Debugging and incident response become dramatically faster.
- Users can replay exactly what an agent did and why a failure occurred.
- Provides the foundation for analytics, SLA verification, and governance.

## Scope and Design
- Persist normalized agent events with stable schemas and redacted payloads.
- Provide cursor-based API access for efficient playback.
- UI timeline with filtering, grouping, and export.
- Retention and sampling controls for storage safety.

## Proposed API
- `GET /api/agents/:id/timeline?cursor=&limit=&types=`
- `GET /api/agents/:id/events/:eventId`
- `GET /api/agents/:id/timeline/export?format=json|csv`
- WebSocket channel `agent:{id}:timeline` (optional live feed)

## Data Model (SQLite)
```
agent_events (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  agent_id TEXT NOT NULL,
  type TEXT NOT NULL,          -- state, tool_call, tool_result, output, error, file
  severity TEXT,               -- info|warn|error
  occurred_at TEXT NOT NULL,
  payload TEXT NOT NULL,       -- JSON (redacted)
  correlation_id TEXT,
  session_id TEXT,
  created_at TEXT NOT NULL
)
CREATE INDEX idx_agent_events_agent_time ON agent_events(agent_id, occurred_at DESC);
```

## Implementation Plan
1. Add `agent_events` schema and migration.
2. Create `agent-event.service.ts` to normalize + store events.
3. Extend BaseDriver emit pipeline to forward events to service.
4. Add redaction layer for payloads (reuse audit redaction).
5. Implement retention policy (size and age based).
6. Build API routes with cursor pagination.
7. Add UI timeline page + filters + event detail drawer.
8. Add export endpoint and UI action.
9. Optional: WebSocket live feed for real-time replay.

## Testing
- Unit: event normalization, redaction, cursor pagination.
- Integration: event ingestion from driver state transitions.
- E2E: timeline page renders events and filters properly.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Timeline shows all key agent events in order.
- Cursor pagination is stable and deterministic.
- Payloads are redacted (no secrets in UI/export).
- Retention policy keeps DB bounded.

## Dependencies

Introduce automated recovery workflows for agents (auto-interrupt, restart, pause, notify) driven by configurable runbooks. This reduces manual babysitting when agents hang or error repeatedly.

## Why This Is Accretive
- Improves reliability and reduces operator load.
- Shortens time-to-recovery for common failure modes.
- Makes behavior predictable and auditable.

## Scope and Design
- Define runbook rules: condition -> action (with cooldowns).
- Conditions: stuck state duration, error-rate threshold, cost overrun, context health, reservation conflicts.
- Actions: interrupt, restart, pause, downgrade model, notify.
- Policy store with per-workspace overrides.

## Proposed API
- `GET /api/runbooks` / `POST /api/runbooks` / `PUT /api/runbooks/:id`
- `POST /api/agents/:id/recover` (manual trigger)
- `GET /api/agents/:id/recovery-history`

## Data Model (SQLite)
```
agent_runbooks (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  name TEXT NOT NULL,
  enabled INTEGER DEFAULT 1,
  condition TEXT NOT NULL,   -- JSON
  action TEXT NOT NULL,      -- JSON
  cooldown_seconds INTEGER,
  created_at TEXT,
  updated_at TEXT
)

agent_recovery_history (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  agent_id TEXT NOT NULL,
  runbook_id TEXT,
  action TEXT NOT NULL,
  reason TEXT,
  executed_at TEXT NOT NULL
)
```

## Implementation Plan
1. Runbook schema + CRUD service.
2. Condition evaluator (rules engine) with cooldown.
3. Recovery executor (interrupt/restart/pause/downgrade).
4. Scheduler that evaluates agents at fixed interval.
5. Add audit logging + recovery history.
6. Build UI editor + runbook list.
7. Add manual trigger button to agent view.

## Testing
- Unit: rule evaluation, cooldown, action selection.
- Integration: simulated stuck agent triggers recovery.
- E2E: create runbook, trigger and verify status change.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Stuck agents recover automatically per policy.
- Recovery actions are logged and visible.
- Cooldowns prevent repeated thrashing.
- Manual trigger works with same runbook actions.

## Dependencies
- Agent health and state tracking (prefer integration with composite health score).","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:26:16.900718261Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T03:26:33.127393460Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-hrs1t","depends_on_id":"bd-1x215","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:26:33.127333148Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

-{"id":"bd-jk8ct","title":"Durable WebSocket Event Replay and Backfill","description":"## Overview
Persist WebSocket events with short-term retention so clients can request backfill after reconnects. This eliminates data loss during flaky connections and enables reliable UIs.

## Why This Is Accretive
- Fixes missed updates on transient disconnects.
- Improves trust in dashboards and real-time views.
- Enables deterministic replay for debugging.

## Scope and Design
- Event log per channel with cursor-based replay.
- Configurable retention (time and size).
- Server emits replay on connect with last cursor.
- Client passes last cursor on reconnect.

## Proposed API
- `GET /api/ws/replay?channel=...&cursor=...&limit=...`
- WebSocket handshake accepts `cursor` query param.
- Server response includes `cursorExpired` flag.

## Data Model
```
ws_event_log (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  channel TEXT NOT NULL,
  cursor INTEGER NOT NULL,
  payload TEXT NOT NULL,
  created_at TEXT NOT NULL
)
CREATE INDEX idx_ws_event_log_channel_cursor ON ws_event_log(channel, cursor);
```

## Implementation Plan
1. Add event log schema + migration.
2. Create `ws-event-log.service.ts` with append + replay.
3. Integrate publish path to persist events (with sampling toggle).
4. Add replay API endpoint and cursor expiration handling.
5. Update websocket client to send last cursor on reconnect.
6. Add retention cleanup job.

## Testing
- Unit: append/replay logic, cursor bounds.
- Integration: publish + reconnect backfill.
- E2E: disconnect/reconnect scenario recovers missed events.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Reconnect yields complete event history since last cursor.
- Cursor expiration returns clean full refresh path.
- Retention keeps DB bounded and does not impact latency.

## Dependencies
- WebSocket hub and channel system.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:26:51.803937938Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T03:26:51.803937938Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

+{"id":"bd-qby4w","title":"Checkpoint Diff Viewer","description":"## Overview

Provide a diff view between checkpoints to understand what changed (files, context, decisions) between two moments in an agent session.

## Background and Rationale

Checkpoints exist, but there is no easy way to compare them. A diff viewer makes regression analysis, auditing, and handoff reviews dramatically faster.

## Scope

- Compute diffs between checkpoints
- Store summarized diff metadata
- UI diff viewer with filtering
- API to request diffs

## Diff Types

- File changes (added/modified/deleted)
- Context deltas (tokens + summary)
- Tool call deltas
- Decision notes (if present)

## API

- `GET /api/checkpoints/:id/diff/:otherId`

## UI

- Side-by-side diff view
- File list with status icons
- Search and filter by file or tool type

## Implementation Tasks

1. Add diff computation service (text + JSON)
2. Cache diff summaries for common pairs
3. Extend checkpoint API with diff endpoint
4. Build UI diff viewer with file navigation
5. Add tool-call and context delta panels

## Testing

- Unit: diff computation for JSON and text
- Integration: diff endpoint returns stable output
- E2E: select two checkpoints and view diff

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Diff view loads within 2s for typical sessions
- [ ] File-level changes are accurate
- [ ] Context and tool deltas are visible
- [ ] UI supports search/filter","notes":"Testing note: E2E coverage should use the existing Playwright TestLogger/StructuredReporter to capture console, network, WS, and traces; emit deterministic log bundles for debugging.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:31:13.368339781Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T03:35:32.996590311Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-qby4w","depends_on_id":"bd-1qxhc","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:32:55.088302839Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

+{"id":"bd-s3ko9","title":"Trace ID and Correlation Context","description":"## Overview

Introduce end-to-end trace IDs that propagate across HTTP, WebSocket, agent sessions, and tool calls for reliable debugging and log correlation.

Investigations are difficult when logs, events, and UI views cannot be tied together. A consistent trace ID improves root-cause analysis and enables richer diagnostics.

## Scope

- Generate trace IDs at entry points
- Propagate through services, events, and logs
- Surface trace IDs in UI and API responses
- Search by trace ID

## Implementation Tasks

1. Add trace middleware for HTTP and WS
2. Extend logging to include traceId and spanId
3. Propagate traceId through agent driver events
4. Expose traceId in API responses and UI
5. Add search filter by traceId

## Testing

- Unit: trace propagation through services
- Integration: trace appears in logs + WS events
- E2E: UI displays trace and supports search

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Every request has a traceId
- [ ] Logs and events share the same traceId
- [ ] UI can filter by traceId
- [ ] TraceId included in support bundles","notes":"Testing note: E2E coverage should use the existing Playwright TestLogger/StructuredReporter to capture console, network, WS, and traces; emit deterministic log bundles for debugging.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:32:37.415464442Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T03:36:25.569286769Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
